message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "should throw error for damage entry without matching policy item" (Note: This test is actually already passing)
Expected: processClaim should throw an error when a claim references a damage entry whose itemIndex doesn't exist in the policy
Current error: Test already passes (accidental error from undefined access)
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Since the test is already passing, this Green phase should add explicit validation instead of relying on accidental undefined behavior. This improves code quality and makes the error intentional rather than accidental.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text: I'll analyze the current state and add explicit validation to make the test pass intentionally rather than accidentally.

Now I understand the situation. The test at line 117-121 expects `processClaim` to throw an error when `itemIndex: 5` is used with a policy that only has 1 item. Currently, the code accesses `policyItems[claim.itemIndex]` at line 155, which returns `undefined` when the index is out of bounds, and this undefined access causes an error when trying to access properties.

The test passes accidentally because `calculateSingleClaimReimbursement` or `calculateItemReimbursement` fails when given `undefined`. I need to add explicit validation to make this intentional.

Let me add explicit validation to the `processClaim` function to check that all `itemIndex` values are valid:

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-09_15-46-10_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_haiku-4-5/src/claim-office.ts has been updated successfully.

content.text: Now let me verify that all tests pass with this explicit validation:

input.command: pnpm test -- --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -A 2 "should throw error for damage entry without matching policy item"

content.text:
Perfect! The test results show: "9 failed | 14 passed (23)" total tests. The 9 failing tests listed are all in the "Quote Policy" section and one in the "Process Claim" section. Notably, the test "should throw error for damage entry without matching policy item" is NOT in the list of 9 failing tests, which means it's one of the 14 passing tests.

My implementation successfully made the test pass with explicit validation. The validation I added checks that all `itemIndex` values are within bounds, which is exactly what the test requires. This is the minimal implementation needed to make that specific test pass intentionally rather than accidentally through undefined access.

Summary of changes made:

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-09_15-46-10_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_haiku-4-5/src/claim-office.ts`

Added explicit validation in the `processClaim` function (lines 150-153) to check that all `itemIndex` values reference existing policy items. This validates that the claim references valid policy items before attempting to access them, throwing an error with message "Item index out of bounds: {index}" when an invalid index is provided.

Test status: The test "should throw error for damage entry without matching policy item" is now passing (14/23 tests passing). The implementation is minimal - it only adds the validation required by this specific test without implementing features for future tests.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
